This article argues that these responses are sound but conceptually incomplete because they treat authorship mainly as a matter of origination and reward. Drawing on Foucault, Merton, Shapin, and Hardwig, I argue that authorship has long bundled three functions: origination, reward, and custodianship. Generative AI disrupts that bundle by separating origination from the forward-looking human work of defending, developing, teaching, remembering, and answering for knowledge over time. The result is what I call orphaned knowledge: findings that may be true and valuable yet lack any identifiable person committed to keeping them alive. The article makes two contributions. First, it shows that both the credit economy of journals and the property economy of copyright are structured around human knowers, leaving machine-originated knowledge in a double vacuum. Second, it advances a custodial theory of authorship that redefines the author’s signature less as a retrospective claim to credit than as a prospective commitment to steward a finding. On this view, a machine may originate, but only a human or institution can undertake custody. The age of machine discovery therefore forces scholarship to confront a prior question that earlier authorship debates have obscured: not only who made knowledge, but who will keep it.

articles.title: Long-term childhood poverty in Britain: trends and drivers across the 1991-2017 birth cohorts
articles.authors: Selçuk Bedük; Anna Yong
articles.abstract: Any experience of childhood poverty affects life chances, but longer exposure is particularly detrimental to education, health, and future earnings. This study examines trends in long-term childhood poverty in Britain. Using a life-course perspective, we tracked poverty from birth to age ten among 1991–2017 birth cohorts. Our findings show that, on average, 17 per cent of children spent at least half of their childhood in poverty. Long-term poverty affected 25 per cent of those born in the early 1990s, markedly declined to 13–14 per cent for cohorts born after the post-1997 welfare reforms, and rose again to 23 per cent for children born following the 2013 austerity reforms. These trends are driven by shifts in the penalties associated with work-family risk factors, rather than by changes in their prevalence. These shifts in penalties reflect broader changes in redistribution and predistribution. The decline in the 1990s was largely due to rising employment and earnings in low-income households, whereas the post-austerity surge stems from reduced redistribution. For cohorts born in the 2000s, social transfers played a substantial role in containing long-term poverty despite worsening predistribution. Overall, the findings show that long-term childhood poverty is a major challenge in Britain and highlight the need to strengthen redistribution and predistribution.

articles.title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the  research process: Systematic thinking from prior  assumptions to final synthesis
articles.authors: Jo Bjørkli Helgetun; Thibault Coppe
articles.abstract:
In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt 
to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices 
through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of 
research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them 
systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, 
towards a synthesis of the research findings.

articles.title: Contained and Uncontained Experiments
articles.authors: Kevin Munger
articles.abstract: I introduce a typology of political science experiments based on their ``containedness." Contained experiments are those for which it is plausible to ``perform the same procedure" and expect ``the same result." Uncontained experiments are those for which practitioners expect intrinsic variation to dominate; even when performing the same procedure, the goal is to understand how results differ across time and context. I propose three conceptions of containedness: pragmatic (community expectations about replication), complexity (information required to specify the procedure), and temporal (speed of experimentation relative to drift in the phenomenon). Lab experiments are generally the most contained, survey experiments occupy a middle position, and field experiments are the least contained. I provide a diagnostic rubric for evaluating containedness and argue that distinct evidentiary strategies are appropriate at different levels. For highly uncontained experiments, ``replication" is not a coherent goal; knowledge accumulation must proceed through theory rather than induction.

articles.title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
articles.authors: Giulia Suzzi
articles.abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.

articles.title: Hedging Under U.S. Reindustrialization: Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia in an Era of Intensifying U.S.–China Rivalry
articles.authors: Julie Yu-Wen Chen
articles.abstract: Southeast Asian states maintain deep economic ties with both the United States and China, and the region has become a major destination for supply-chain relocation from firms in both powers. Recent U.S. reindustrialization policies—including the extension of elevated tariffs to Southeast Asian exports—pose new challenges to the region's export-led growth model and to U.S. strategic positioning in the Indo-Pacific. Drawing on hedging theory, particularly Cheng-Chwee Kuik's distinction between return-maximizing and risk-contingency behaviors, this paper examines how three manufacturing-oriented, trade-dependent economies—Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia—adjusted their foreign-policy and geoeconomic orientations in response to successive waves of U.S. reindustrialization from 2017 to 2026. Using a theory-driven event-coding approach, the study analyzes 66 verified foreign-policy and geoeconomic events to trace directional tilts toward Washington, Beijing, or neutrality across three U.S. administrations. The findings show that U.S. reindustrialization has not uniformly pushed Southeast Asian states toward China. Instead, different phases of U.S. policy produced qualitatively distinct hedging responses, mediated by structural factors that vary across countries. The analysis also reveals a misalignment between U.S. tariff instruments and the goals of the Indo-Pacific Strategy: tariff shocks create openings for Chinese economic leverage in the very economies U.S. industrial policy seeks to draw closer. The durability of the Indo-Pacific Strategy will depend on whether Washington can align its economic tools with its security ambitions and provide the structural conditions that enable partners to hedge coherently under intensifying great-power competition.

articles.title: eHealth Use among Adults with Chronic Illness in the United States: Health Self-Efficacy and Health Literacy as Pathways to Mental Well-Being
articles.authors: Qianfeng Lu; Wen Jiao; Qiaofei Jovi Wu
articles.abstract: Patients with chronic illness face a considerable mental health burden. However, the pathways through which eHealth use influences mental health in this population remain poorly understood. Drawing on Street's (2003) three-stage model, this study examined the effects of eHealth use on mental health among chronic illness patients, with health self-efficacy as a mediator and health literacy as a moderator. Data from the Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7) were analysed among U.S. adults with chronic illness (N = 2,928) using the PROCESS macro. The direct effect of eHealth use on mental health was insignificant. eHealth use was negatively associated with health self-efficacy, which was then positively linked to mental health. Health literacy moderated the relationship between eHealth use and health self-efficacy, with the negative association significant at lower literacy levels but diminishing and trending positive at higher literacy levels. Consequently, the indirect effect of eHealth use on mental health through health self-efficacy was contingent on health literacy, being negative at lower levels and diminishing as literacy increased. These findings suggest that health literacy determines whether eHealth use burdens patients with chronic illness. Therefore, ensuring adequate patients health literacy is important in eHealth interventions targeting this population.

articles.title: Sinks and Sluices: Contrasting Dynamics in Spatial Assimilation Pathways
articles.authors: Kristian Gade Kjelmann; Anja Jørgensen
articles.abstract:
This study examines how neighbourhoods can be understood through their functional roles in immigrant settlement. Drawing on register data covering internal migration among first-generation immigrants in Denmark (2007–2012), the analysis models migration as networks and applies the Hubs and Authorities (HITS) algorithm to classify neighbourhoods as sinks, denoting areas of prolonged settlement, or sluices, denoting areas of high turnover. These typologies are linked to composite measures of marginalisation and integration, constructed from group-level socioeconomic indicators at settlement and five years later.
Results reveal that the association between neighbourhood role and integration outcomes is neighbourhood-dependent rather than uniform. The same neighbourhood may function as a site of long-term residence for some immigrant groups, while being highly transient for others. We find no overall association between internal migration patterns and socioeconomic integration outcomes at the neighbourhood-level. Neighbourhoods near city centres and on the periphery often display dual functions, indicating transitional dynamics within the urban system. Over time, sinks tend to become more strongly associated with marginalisation, while the role of sluices remains variable.
The findings support a pluralistic understanding of neighbourhoods as dynamic contexts shaped by migration flows, rather than as fixed determinants of outcomes, as integration and spatial mobility are linked for some immigrant groups but not others. Overall, the study highlights the value of treating migration as a network process for capturing the fluid, group-specific geographies of integration.

articles.title: Who Overreports? Regime Support and Preference Falsification in Iran
articles.authors: Daniel L Tavana; Kevan Harris; chi shun fong; Amir Farmanesh
articles.abstract: In authoritarian contexts, individuals often express greater support for the regime in public than they hold in private. This gap---overreporting---is typically treated as a measurement problem rather than a political behavior to be explained. We argue that overreporting deserves greater attention in survey-based studies of regime support and is itself shaped by which social referents are salient to respondents during survey interviews. We develop and test an original theoretical framework that identifies three sources of overreporting: expressive identification, behavioral conformity, and civic resources. Drawing on a pre-registered, nationally representative telephone survey of residents of Iran (N=1,999) with an embedded double list experiment, we estimate regime support from the list experiment at 47 percent---roughly ten percentage points below the 57 percent recorded by direct questioning. Our findings indicate that expressive identification matters most: national pride and religiosity both predict substantial overreporting. Preference falsification in Iran is shaped less by the coercion citizens face from the state than by the identity-based attachments the regime encourages---findings with implications for how scholars measure and interpret regime support in closed societies.

articles.title: The Erosion of Legal Education under Neoliberalism: Rigor, Authority, and Professional Formation in Chile
articles.authors: Edison Carrasco-Jiménez
articles.abstract:
This article examines how the neoliberal transformation of higher education in Chile has
eroded the conditions necessary for rigorous legal education and, consequently, for the ethical and
professional formation of lawyers. Drawing on Marx’s critique of ideology and Nietzsche’s analysis of
moral decadence, the article conceptualizes neoliberalism not merely as an economic arrangement
but as a moral and cultural order shaping universities, students, and legal educators. Using Chile as
a paradigmatic case—given its early and radical adoption of market-based educational reforms—the
analysis shows how managerial governance, the client-student model, weakened academic authority,
and the commodification of learning have undermined the intellectual discipline central to legal
training. The article further argues that the failures of legal education manifest beyond the university,
contributing to a broader institutional fragility visible in recent public scandals involving legal
professionals. By linking philosophical critique, pedagogical analysis, and institutional outcomes, this
study highlights the urgent need to rethink the cultural and ethical foundations of legal education in
neoliberal contexts.

articles.title: Human Brokers in Machine Times: The Network Architecture of Journalism from COVID-19 to the AI Era
articles.authors: James Jaehoon Lee; Jeffrey Layne Blevins; Jeremy D. Gilbert; Joe Germuska
articles.abstract: The dominant narrative of journalism in the digital age has focused on institutional decline and audience fragmentation. However, during the COVID-19 pandemic, we find that news organizations surprisingly reemerged as central actors within digital information ecosystems, serving as network brokers between fragmented online communities. Five years later, we conducted a 6,000-respondent cross-national survey to show that news-mediated brokerage remains durable in the era of AI models, but individual content creators share the brokerage role through which distinct publics come into contact. Here we perform a longitudinal analysis of the changing role of news organizations from 2020 to 2025, by combining network analysis of 141 million tweets from 24 million users during 2020, 45 qualitative interviews with younger news consumers across the United States, Nigeria, and India, and a 6000 person YouGov survey fielded in June 2025 across the US, UK, India, Nigeria, Brazil, and South Korea. Using network analysis and the concept of brokerage, we show that journalism operates as a connective layer in network architecture, channeling information flow across ideologically and geographically distinct communities. We identify a platform-native model of journalism that departs from traditional article formats in favor of contextualized, multimodal, and shareable content. Our findings suggest that in the era of digital platforms, the conventional journalistic product of the article gives way to a distributed model of sense-making, in which journalism's value lies in brokering connections rather than delivering monolithic event narratives. By 2025, the brokerage function persists but the brokers themselves have shifted. Individual creators now share the bridging labor that news organizations performed alone in 2020. These findings suggest a new account of journalism as a dynamic connective broker within networked public life and contemporary information ecosystems.

articles.title: bqmm: Bayesian Multilevel Quantile Regression in R
articles.authors: Kailas Venkitasubramanian
articles.abstract:
Quantile regression describes how covariates shift the conditional quantiles of an outcome, not
merely its mean, and is indispensable when effects are heterogeneous across the response distribution.
When data are clustered or longitudinal, a mixed-effects formulation is needed. I present bqmm,
an R package for Bayesian multilevel quantile regression built on the asymmetric Laplace working
likelihood and Stan. The package offers an lme4-style formula interface with nested and crossed
random effects, optional LKJ-correlated random effects, estimation of one or several quantiles in
a single call, post-hoc non-crossing rearrangement, and a transparent menu of fixed-effect interval
methods — the naive posterior, the Yang–Wang–He (2016) sandwich correction, and the infinitesimal
jackknife — because the asymmetric Laplace likelihood is misspecified and naive credible intervals
can be invalid. The paper describes the model and software design, illustrates usage on longitudinal
growth data, summarises a validation study (parameter recovery, simulation-based calibration, and
a coverage study), and compares bqmm with related software.

articles.title: Personality Emergence and Adaptive Functioning: An Enactive, Complex-Dynamical  Systems Account
articles.authors: Nicol Alejandra Arellano-Véliz
articles.abstract:
Enactive psychiatry conceptualizes mental disorders as maladaptive modes of functioning that 
emerge within the recursive coupling between an embodied agent and their world. This chapter 
advances an integrative framework synthesizing enactive principles with complex dynamical systems 
theory to illustrate how personality operates as the foundational architecture of these functional 
modes. Personality is conceptualized as an evolving topography of attractor landscapes or stylistic 
regularities, structuring multi-level sense-making across nested timescales. Fundamental to this 
account is adaptive flexibility, the metastable capacity of a system to balance structural coherence 
and environmental exploration. The multi-level topology of individual differences is mapped 
through the traits and facets of the Five-Factor Model, with developmental pathways traced to early 
temperament and interaction patterns, reframed here as sedimented relational topologies of 
structural coupling. Personality difficulties or maladaptive functioning is understood as global 
alterations in control topology, characterized either by pathological rigidity and high inertia, or 
systemic fragmentation and high instability. Finally, this framework operationalizes enactive theory 
through an empirical turn, utilizing real-time tracking methodologies and time series analyses to 
capture intrapersonal and interpersonal coordination. Clinical change is comprehended as context
sensitive perturbations that facilitate systemic reorganization, providing a conceptually coherent and 
clinically actionable science of the person.

articles.title: Getting Creeped Out? Open Science, Qualitative Methods, and the Dangers of Positivism Creep
articles.authors: Thomas Anthony Graves; Madeleine Pownall; Annayah Miranda Beatrice Prosser
articles.abstract: Many developments to reform the research landscape have occurred over the past decade. These changes have been made with broad goals to improve the ‘openness’ of research and often assumed to be ‘methodologically -agnostic’; that is, they ostensibly have benefits for all researchers occupying all epistemological and methodological positions. ‘Open science’ initiatives such as study pre-registration (i.e., specifying research aims and analytical plan ahead of data access), open data sharing, open-access publication,  and open materials sharing are becoming increasingly mainstream across many fields within social research and the natural sciences. While there has been much criticism of these interventions, largely from the qualitative research community, we want to draw attention to a troubling trend in the promotion of open science: the leaking of standards relevant only to quantitative research to all paradigms. Or, as others refer to it, “positivism creep”. Here, we situate positivism creep (i.e., the creeping of positivist conventions to all research) within research policy, we highlight its increasing prevalence within open science reforms, and we warn against a future which could alienate many non-positivist scholars. We argue that the primary framing of open science as the pursuit of reproducibility and objectivity risks promoting positivism creep in the social sciences and humanities. In particular, we suggest that overly strict open research requirements placed by funders may reduce the range and variety of epistemological positions that can be taken by researchers, with particularly deleterious effects for qualitative researchers.

articles.title: Beyond Individual Vulnerability: A European, Critical Work and Organizational Psychology Perspective on Work Addiction in the Digital Workplace.
articles.authors: Alessandro Reati
articles.abstract: Adopting a European vantage point, both empirically and with respect to the regulatory context, this position paper argues that work addiction in digitally mediated work cannot be adequately understood as a matter of individual vulnerability or as a mechanical effect of technology. Drawing on behavioural-addiction models and on critical work and organizational psychology, it advances a psychosocial and practice-oriented reading in which compulsive work emerges from the interaction of personal dispositions, organizational cultures, role demands, digital tools, and recognition systems. The paper distinguishes work addiction from work engagement and from heavy work investment, locating the construct in the combination of excess and compulsion rather than in working hours. It then reframes digital tools (email, messaging, collaborative platforms, and the smartphone) not as neutral instruments but as facilitators, amplifiers, and maintenance environments, connecting the argument to constructs the field has already named, including workplace telepressure, low psychological detachment, technostress, and the autonomy paradox. The argument is extended to the still-fragmented, partly worker-led adoption of artificial intelligence at work, drawing on European and OECD evidence that points to task intensification rather than wholesale displacement, and to governance that lags adoption. A central claim is that organizations frequently reward continuous availability as professionalism, normalizing compulsion as a virtue and obscuring it as a psychosocial risk. Against this backdrop, recent European and Italian developments on the right to disconnect are read as encouraging but partial signals. The paper closes with propositions for a critical-practice agenda, framing the right to disconnect as an organizational competence rather than an individual hygiene practice.

articles.title: The Topology of Cyberspace and Cybercrime Journeys: A Framework for Analyzing Online Offender Mobility
articles.authors: Asier Moneva; Stijn Ruiter; Wim Bernasco
articles.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for analyzing online offender mobility. Drawing on multidisciplinary insights, we extend two established criminological models for studying offline offending—the geometry of crime and crime journeys—by adapting their core concepts to cyberspace and developing a topology in which the cyber place is defined and becomes the unit of analysis. Just as offenders travel before, during, and after committing crime offline, we argue they also undertake trips before, during, and after committing cybercrime, and that like offline journeys these cybercrime journeys comprise identifiable and measurable components. We further distinguish between human journeys (what offenders perceive) and data journeys (what happens to the information they transmit). We explicitly demonstrate how concepts from the geometry of crime and crime journeys translate from offline to online crime and mobility. The proposed approach enables the systematic formulation of research questions and the measurement of behavioral patterns, facilitating the generation and accumulation of knowledge on cybercrime offending. The paper illustrates the framework’s theoretical relevance within environmental criminology and its practical application for cybercrime analysis through concrete examples, including an empirical human journey into web hacking as a proof-of-concept.

articles.title: Public perceptions of climate policy fairness and effectiveness across climate policy types and domains in 15 countries
articles.authors: Nina Leonie Frings; Ulf J.J. Hahnel
articles.abstract: Individuals are more supportive of climate policies when they think the measures are fair and effective. This evidence, however, is primarily based on data from Western samples, leaving it open how public effectiveness and fairness perceptions vary across geographical contexts and how they are shaped by regional indicators. Here, we aim to address these questions, investigating climate policy effectiveness and fairness perceptions across four policy types and two policy domains in 15 countries, covering all continents (N=6’052). Effectiveness and fairness ratings varied between policy types and domains, and this pattern was relatively stable across nations. However, individuals in the Global South were overall more optimistic about the effectiveness of climate policies and evaluated them as generally fairer compared to the Global North. Linking psychological and contextual data further shows that effectiveness and fairness perceptions were higher in countries that are more vulnerable to climate change impacts and lower in countries with higher per capita emissions. Moreover, the impact of psychological variables such as climate change concern and political orientation on both outcomes varied as a function of national context. Taken together, our findings point to important geographical asymmetries in the extent to which the public perceives climate mitigation policies as effective and fair, shaped by how much countries contribute to and are affected by climate change.

articles.title: What voters think and feel about a climate law: cognitive–affective mental models during a Swiss national referendum
articles.authors: Nina Leonie Frings; Julius Fenn; Samuel Aeschbach; Rui Mata; Ulf J.J. Hahnel
articles.abstract: Lacking public support remains a major barrier to ambitious climate policy, yet little is known about how citizens mentally represent specific policy proposals and whether or how these representations change over time. We assessed voters’ cognitive– affective mental models of a real-world climate policy proposal, the 2023 Swiss Climate Protection Law, at two time points preceding a national referendum on the policy. A sample of Swiss voters (N=254) drew their mental models by adding their own cognitive associations and affective evaluations towards the law one month before and immediately prior to the referendum. Opposers were more likely to make reference to financial or personal costs and to rate concepts more negatively, whereas supporters were more likely to make reference to climate and future protection concepts and to rate concepts more positively. While the cognitive associations differed across voter groups, the complexity of mental models, indicated by the number of provided concepts and their interconnectedness, did not. Both opposers and supporters reported more cost-related concepts at the second time point but there was no strong evidence of increased complexity or changes in affective evaluations over time. Crucially, affective evaluations were the strongest predictor of final policy support relative to cognitive associations. These results suggest that while political campaigns may shape the considerations citizens associate with a policy, policy support does not necessarily reflect differences in the complexity of these representations and may be more closely associated with their affective evaluation than with their cognitive content.

articles.title: Examining Measurements of Fear of Crime Across Surveys: Content, Cognitive Processes, and Psychometric Properties
articles.authors: Jeanette Melin; Marika Wenemark; Magnus Johansson
articles.abstract:
Purpose: To enhance the understanding of different survey questions and the opportunities they provide to measure fear of crime as a latent trait, examining both crime-type and consequence items.
Methods: Items from five surveys used in the Stockholm Region were coded into content areas. Cognitive interviews (n = 19) were analysed for response processes. Rasch analyses were conducted on the Swedish Crime Survey (n = 4,264) and the City of Stockholm Crime Survey (n = 7,333).
Results: The surveys covered overlapping topics but used distinct phrasings. Response comprehension was generally adequate, yet some terms were interpreted differently. Respondents found it difficult to isolate fear of crime from other motives in the consequence items. There was no clear advantage to either frequency- or consequence-framed items, whereas intensity stems imposed a lower response burden. Psychometrically, unidimensional solutions were hard to achieve for either crime type or consequence item sets. The evidence suggests heterogeneity across population groups.
Conclusions: Current practice involves both strengths and weaknesses. This study suggests paths toward enhanced measurement of the fear of crime. Measuring fear of crime as a single, invariant latent trait is far from straightforward. The heterogeneity of responses across population groups indicates that different groups evaluate their fear of crime types and consequences differently, challenging the assumption of unidimensionality. However, the cognitive interviews suggest ways to clarify item wording and reduce respondent burden.

articles.title: Empirical Verification of an Asset Dynamics Model in Open Economies: An Equilibrium Theory Endogenizing Imbalances Bridging Steady-State Conditions and Social Adaptation
articles.authors: Kazuhito Kitamura
articles.abstract:
The general equilibrium theory, upon which mainstream modern macroeconomics relies, fails to adequately explain the realities enveloping the contemporary economy, including persistent global imbalances. In contrast, our previous studies (Kitamura, 2025, 2026) have proposed a theoretical model based on "asset dynamics" that conceptualizes the global economy as a dissipative structure woven by the concentration and diffusion of funds. We understand it as an "equilibrium theory endogenizing imbalances" where economies, each harboring its own imbalances, maintain a balance by compensating for each other's surpluses and deficits.
This paper statistically verifies the empirical validity of this theoretical framework using multi-source macroeconomic data and socio-psychological indicators. Targeting approximately 80 countries for which data are available, we redefine a socio-psychological indicator based on a large-scale international survey regarding time value, patience, and other attributes, as a proxy for the divergence between the real interest rate and the time preference rate—which constitutes one side of the steady-state condition derived from our theoretical model. Conducting a regression analysis using the structural terms that constitute the right-hand side of the conditional equation, namely population changes, capital transfer, and asset preference, we obtained statistically significant results, including the sign conditions. Furthermore, by utilizing these estimation results, we visualized the dissipative structure that weaves the global economy, rendering the contrasting positions and interdependence of the two major economic superpowers as data-driven facts, such as the United States as a self-organizing economy and China as a dissipative economy.
The contributions of this paper provide an empirical foundation for a new macroeconomic paradigm that reframes imbalances not as an undesirable state to be eliminated, but endogenously explains them as the energy required for the global economic system to maintain its vitality.

articles.title: Device Effects and Response Quality in Online Surveys: Evidence from Czech GGS Paradata
articles.authors: Daniel Dvořák
articles.abstract: This study examines device effects on response behaviour and data quality in the Czech Generations and Gender Survey, Wave II, using keystroke-level paradata from a fully mobile-optimised CAWI instrument. We compare respondents completing the survey on PCs/laptops and smartphones across indicators capturing cognitive load, satisficing, and respondent effort, including response latency, completion time, answer changes, page revisits, item nonresponse, midpoint use, “don’t know” responses, and straightlining. Descriptive analyses and multivariate models controlling for age, gender, and education show that most device differences are small or negligible. Smartphone use is not associated with longer completion times or systematically lower engagement once demographic composition is accounted for. The only robust and substantively meaningful device effect is a higher incidence of straightlining among smartphone respondents, alongside a very small increase in item nonresponse. Education consistently attenuates device-related differences. Overall, the findings indicate broad comparability across devices in a mobile-optimised survey, while highlighting specific behavioural domains where smartphones continue to shape response processes.

articles.title: Upfront Pricing or In-Game Purchases? Monetizing Content in Video Games
articles.authors: James Campbell
articles.abstract: A video game developer can monetize its content (maps, themes, modes) in two ways: upfront pricing, a single high up-front price for the game with all content included, or in-game purchases, a free or cheap base with each content piece sold separately. We model this as a choice between bundling and separately selling a content stream and ask when in-game purchases dominate. Upfront pricing wins when content tastes are dispersed and weakly correlated; in-game purchases win when tastes are strongly correlated or pieces diﬀer in appeal, when risk-averse players face uncertain content, and when players are present-biased or impulsive. A simulation with all forces active shows that bundling is the benchmark optimum and that each friction shifts the boundary toward in-game purchases, with behavioral forces moving it most. The framework explains why premium console and PC games are still sold up front, while long-lived games, especially mobile games, rely on in-game purchases.

articles.title: Benchmarking the Personalization Capabilities of Large Language Models
articles.authors: Vinay Aggarwal; Yaman K Singla; Ashutosh Srivastava; Siddharth Yedlapati; Shashwat Dixit; Jitendra Ajmera; Balaji Krishnamurthy
articles.abstract:
Personalization, the act of varying a message to induce action from a specific receiver while keeping sender, channel, and time fixed, has a long tradition in psychology and marketing as a two-party problem in which sender and receiver have independent objectives. Large language models remove the bounded-inventory constraint of classical retrieval-and-ranking approaches by generating a continuum of message variants conditioned on inferred receiver state, raising the question of how well current models perform personalization in the classical sense. Existing LLM personalization benchmarks measure sender-side adaptation, in which the receiver is the same user the model is serving. The two-party question, whether a generated message induces its intended action in a third party, has been investigated only through A/B tests and small-scale human studies that cannot be re-run against a new model on demand. We adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to generative agents and instantiate the formulation in sales, where receiver actions are routinely logged against the outreach that induced them. We release SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories spanning 22 industries and approximately 200 enterprises, served through a temporally constrained simulation that prevents future-data leakage. Across frontier LLMs and deep-research agents, we observe a consistent personalization plateau and on a Fortune 100 tech cohort no model statistically separates successful from unsuccessful outreach. A field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives validates the framework, with 48 percent of model-generated content rated immediately useful and senior-expert agreement at Pearson 0.82. We release SDR-Arena and SDR-Bench publicly to support reproducible study of generative personalization
at scale.

articles.title: Aligning Curriculum, Assessment, and Cultural Contexts: A UbD-CRP Model for Chinese Language Learning in Sri Lanka
articles.authors: Chathuni Weerakoon; Syed Tahir Abbas
articles.abstract: This study aims to provide a new conceptual model for the integration of Understanding by Design (UbD) and Culturally Responsive Pedagogy (CRP) within Chinese as a Foreign Language (CFL) teaching in Sri Lanka. Culturally misaligned measures, imported instructional materials, and teacher-centered pedagogy are challenges in CFL teaching. The framework focuses on embedding culture to define desired outcomes, measures, and instructional activities. The three-stage UbD model (Desired Results, Evidence, Learning Plan) is modified to encompass the design of transfer-based goals and authentic activities, as well as a gradual learning framework to supplement the Sinhala and Tamil languages and cultures of the learners. The model is sensitive to the concepts of transfer and culture, as well as the engagement of learners. The model is user-friendly for educators, instructional designers, and policy makers. Potential outcomes include the crafting of authentic, culturally embedded, and meaningful instructional activities with a design pattern based on solid research. The model is also responsive to the calls for future research that include empirical studies, assessment of learners, and longitudinal studies focusing on the transfer of skills. It provides a comprehensive, integrated approach to teaching CFL in non-heritage contexts, based on a solid theoretical foundation.

articles.title: When, and how much, might effect estimates be biased when analysing mixed-mode survey data? The roles of mode effects, mode selection, and mode split.
articles.authors: Georgia D Tomova; Richard J. Silverwood; Liam Wright
articles.abstract: Survey data are increasingly collected using mixed-mode designs (e.g. personal interview and web questionnaire). Little is known, however, about the extent to which this introduces bias in subsequent analyses, or whether simply including mode as a covariate addresses it. Using data simulations, we identified the conditions under which mode effects (mode measurement differences) and mode selection (respondent differences) introduce bias, complementing this with empirical illustrations using mixed-mode data from the 1958 National Child Development Study. In simulations, absent mode selection, substantial bias arose only from unusually large mode effects, but was amplified when mode selection was present. Controlling for mode under strong mode selection introduced substantial bias, including sign-reversal of the estimate. Bias was more pronounced when the sample was split equally between modes. The direction and size of bias will depend on the direction of all effects. In our empirical illustration, the results were largely unchanged after controlling for mode, possibly reflecting weaker mode selection and mode effects in these data. These findings highlight that, while possible, substantial bias from mixed-mode designs may be unlikely in many practical settings. However, the risk of bias, and the appropriate strategy to address it, should be considered on an analysis-specific basis.

articles.title: Buying time, doing family? Domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden
articles.authors: Betiel Selassie; Elien Dalman; Lisa Eklund; Therese Nilsson
articles.abstract:
Domestic outsourcing is often presented as a way to reduce unpaid work, ease work-family conflict, and promote gender equality. However, it remains unclear whether outsourcing domestic services changes how individuals divide their time between paid work, housework, childcare, and other forms of unpaid work. This paper examines the relationship between domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden, a strong dual-earner context in which the RUT tax deduction subsidizes half of domestic services costs. We link the 2021 Swedish Time Use Survey to administrative register data on household-level use of RUT services to reliably capture outsourcing incidence and intensity. This linkage allows us to examine how domestic outsourcing associates with time spent on paid work and types of unpaid work, and how these associations differ by gender and parenthood status.
The results indicate that outsourcing associates with differences in time allocation above a relatively high threshold of regular use, but that the relationship between outsourcing and time use depends on family type. Regular domestic outsourcing associates with a pattern consistent with time allocation theory among individuals without resident children. Non-parenting men and women who regularly outsource spend substantially more time on paid work and less time on unpaid work than otherwise similar non-outsourcers. Reductions in unpaid work are concentrated in routine housework, the activity most directly substitutable. Among parents, by contrast, regular outsourcing does not associate with more time in paid work nor clear reductions in total unpaid work. Mothers show some reduction in routine housework, accompanied by increases in non-routine housework, while fathers in regularly outsourcing households do not spend less time on unpaid work, including routine housework.
The results suggest that domestic outsourcing cannot be understood simply as buying time for paid work. Among parents it may supplement rather than replace own unpaid work and may thus help sustain intensive combinations of employment, caregiving and other parts of domestic life. The analysis also reveals discrepancies between register-based and self-reported measures of outsourcing. The study contributes to research on gender, outsourcing, and work-family dynamics by showing that the meaning of “buying time” varies by gender and parenthood.

articles.title: Monetary-Fiscal Nexus and Price Level Dynamics in Ghana: An ARDL Analysis of Public Debt Servicing, Short-Term Debt, and Inflation (1980-2020)
articles.authors: Isaac Nyame; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
This study investigates the macroeconomic consequences of public debt composition and servicing obligations on price stability in Ghana from 1980 to 2020. Operating within a dual-model framework, the empirical analysis distinguishes between the cash-flow effects of public debt servicing (PDS) and the maturity-risk impacts of short-term public debt (SPD). The econometric methodology employs Dickey-Fuller Generalised Least Squares (DF-GLS) and Phillips-Perron unit root tests alongside an Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) bounds testing approach to accommodate mixed integration orders among the variables. The bounds test confirms a stable, long-run cointegrating relationship in both the PDS and SPD specifications.
Empirically, the study reveals that PDS exerts a statistically significant negative effect on domestic inflation in both the short run (coefficient = -0.81, p < 0.10) and the long run (coefficient = -0.93, p < 0.05). Conversely, SPD remains statistically insignificant across both temporal horizons. These findings challenge the conventional predictions of the fiscal theory of the price level, suggesting instead that heavy debt-servicing requirements contract aggregate demand by crowding out productive public expenditure. Among the control variables, exchange rate depreciation acts as the dominant positive driver of price levels, while economic growth serves as a robust dampening factor. The error correction terms (-0.87 and -0.90) indicate a rapid speed of adjustment toward long-run equilibrium. These findings suggest that debt restructuring should be coupled with careful demand management, and emphasize that stabilizing local currency value should be prioritized over adjusting debt maturity profiles to maintain overall economic stability.

articles.title: COORDINATION AND GROWTH IN TOURISM: When self-interest does not suffice
articles.authors: Moisés Navarro-Sánchez; Federico Inchausti Sintes
articles.abstract: Private goods and common goods coexist in tourism. However, the nature of the latter means that self-interest alone cannot guarantee their optimal provision. Hence, coordination emerges as a necessary strategy to reconcile both. The analysis shows that, with coordination, a virtuous circle emerges between common goods (public incentive) and tourism (private incentive), becoming more intense and important with tourism-driven economic development. Coordination also allows a transition toward high-quality tourism (crowding out low-quality tourism), which is necessary to compensate for the lack of productivity in this sector. Without coordination, there is room for a tourism development trap or economic growth reversal. Finally, we identify a “Quality Paradox”: quality improvements might jeopardize economic growth by triggering a price hike, reducing overall competitiveness.

articles.title: Inclusive by design: a three-pillar framework for equitable online conferences
articles.authors: Ebi Antony George; Natacha Rossi; Alexis Buatois; Valentin Lecheval
articles.abstract: Scientific conferences remain central to scholarly communication, yet participation is constrained by several systemic barriers. Although online conferencing expanded rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic, there remains limited evidence regarding which organisational practices effectively support equitable participation and sustained engagement. Here, we examine Animal Behaviour Live (ABL), a multi-year international online conference series, as a case study in inclusive conference design. We propose a framework structured around three organisational pillars – who participates, who presents, and who organises – combined with an iterative evaluation of participant engagement. For each pillar, we identify systemic barriers to inclusion and discuss targeted organisational strategies derived from our experience across four conference editions. Synthesising demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations of abstract selection outcomes, we show that ABL sustains broad international participation and long-term asynchronous engagement while increasing representation across geographic regions, gender, and career stages. We further demonstrate that a continent-stratified abstract selection process actively mitigates psychological self-selection biases, such as a gendered confidence gap in format preference. This process also outperformed random or score-only selection approaches in simultaneously balancing scientific quality, gender representation, geographic diversity, and inclusion of researchers self-reporting lower access to the international scientific community. Our findings establish that equitable representation in online conferences does not emerge by default and provide an evidence-informed framework with actionable strategies for designing more inclusive international scientific conferences.

articles.title: Risky Business: On the politicisation of risk in European AI standardization
articles.authors: Daniel Schneiß
articles.abstract:
Technical standards are supposed to become a key material infrastructure and regulatory mecha-nism in global AI governance. Nonetheless, there are only few empirically grounded studies, researching the inherent interest politics and value conflicts within the design and development process of AI standards. Drawing on fieldwork conducted between 2023 and 2026, including interviews with standardisation experts, regulators, and standards-developing organisations, analysis of standards documents and reports, and participant observation in committees and online communities, this paper examines how AI is translated into technical standards and con-structed as a regulatory object.
In contrast to approaches that emphasise the depoliticising effects of standardisation practices, the findings reveal an emerging politicisation of the field. Rather than merely implementing regulatory objectives, standardisers engage in divergent conflicts over competing visions of AI. Empirically, the paper identifies three classification conflicts concerning categorisation, measurement, and interference. While risk serves as a central organising principle for classifying AI systems, its practical enactment remains contested and the standardisation process itself remains risky as its outcomes are uncertain and contested. By outlining these conflicts, this paper inter-venes in the growing yet heterogeneous debate on AI governance in practice. The findings suggest that ongoing conflicts within AI standardisation may significantly influence the future implementation, legitimacy, and effectiveness of EU AI governance.

articles.title: What the North Can Learn from the South Sex Work, Self-Organisation, and Extraterritorial Power in the Global South. A Theoretical Synthesis.
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the
Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier
companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is
decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state
would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the
protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves -
as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks,
often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and
movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker
activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in
India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's
analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the
degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest
predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same
extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law
operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the
PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an
alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR
itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made
themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and
South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence
from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer
efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process
serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment.

Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/uc57b_v1

articles.title: Unpacking Coloniality from the South: Gendered Intimacies and Affective Contracts in Peruvian Migrant Labor in São Paulo, Brazil
articles.authors: Lorena Izaguirre
articles.abstract: This article addresses a central question: how are colonial hierarchies of race, class, gender, and geography mobilized within intimate, co-ethnic networks to legitimize labor exploitation? Drawing on the case of Peruvian migrants in São Paulo’s artisanal workshops—spaces that double as homes and workplaces—I argue that exploitation is underpinned by a distinctly gendered mechanism: the instrumentalization of affective ties. Combining the lenses of the coloniality of power (Quijano, 2000) and the coloniality of gender (Lugones, 2008), I propose the notion of an unwritten “affective contract” that anchors inequality, particularly for Indigenous women. Based on biographical interviews, the analysis unfolds in four parts: (a) recruitment processes and the dynamics of dependent mobilities; (b) intimacy as a gendered mechanism of labor control; (c) the role of affective contracts in shaping labor relations; and (d) the blurring of family and work boundaries. The article extends coloniality theory by tracing its intimate, gendered micro-mechanisms and contributes to migration studies by showing how kinship and paisano networks reproduce historical inequalities in South–South migration.

articles.title: Was der Norden vom Süden lernen kann Sexarbeit, Selbstorganisation und extraterritoriale Macht im Globalen Süden. Eine theoretische Synthese.
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the
Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier
companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is
decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state
would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the
protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves -
as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks,
often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and
movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker
activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in
India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's
analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the
degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest
predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same
extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law
operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the
PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an
alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR
itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made
themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and
South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence
from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer
efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process
serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment.

Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/df43j_v1

articles.title: Data-driven Chinese investment and trade in Europe beyond Huawei and TikTok
articles.authors: Lea Schneidemesser
articles.abstract:
Data access and related security implications have become a frequent concern in Chinese companies’
global expansion. This paper argues that the intensifying debate over the national security implications of
Chinese companies’ data access abroad lacks a more nuanced understanding of the types of data accessed.
Using a typology of four data types: transaction, product, process, and transmission and storage data,
access to data by Chinese companies in Germany is empirically investigated. The analysis shows that
venture capital investments, mergers and acquisitions, greenfield investments, and trade provide access to
different types of data. Trade is the most important internationalization strategy for access to data, and
product data is the most frequently accessed data type. This has several implications for the debate over
the security risks posed by Chinese companies’ access to data in Europe. Trade requires little physical
presence in Germany; data access can be achieved without ownership of large-scale physical infrastructure.
It reveals a mismatch between the predominant regulatory focus on mergers and acquisitions and trade
as the primary strategy for data access, which is less regulated. Trade, as the central data access strategy,
also implies that much depends on the market success of China’s products and services.

articles.title: Politics of Association and Evaluative Logics in the Climate Contrarian Blogosphere
articles.authors: Mathew Toll
articles.abstract: This study provides an investigation of the processes of legitimation and evaluative logics of the climate contrarian blogosphere. Attention is a scarce resource in information rich environments. Drawing on theoretical frameworks from Bourdieu’s Field Theory and Science and Technology Studies, hyperlinks are conceptualised as offering an insight into the enacted epistemological and axiological preferences of climate sceptic bloggers as these digital objects trace who is deemed worthy of attention and what kinds of knowledge practices these targeted sources represent. Data was collected from the blogrolls of 198 climate sceptic and lukewarmer blogs that were sampled by a snowball method to generate a final dataset of 857 nodes/websites with 2628 edges/hyperlinks. These nodes were thematically classified to identify types of websites and sources. Analysis of network composition showed the most prominent sources on the climate contrarian blogosphere were climate sceptic sources (35.1%), right-wing sources (16.7%), and scientific sources (12.3%). Further node-level models and exponential random graph models were employed to estimate how enacted preferences differed by structural position and influenced tie formation processes. This paper contributes to sociological theories of legitimation by demonstrating how evaluative logics in digital fields of struggle can be reconstructed through the relational traces of enacted preference.

articles.title: Exploring the mobilities of Kenyan older people living in informal settlements using a capability approach
articles.authors: Carlo Luiu; Amos Wandera; Vera Bukachi; Rosie Day
articles.abstract: Despite significant discussions on transport inequalities in African cities, older people's mobility needs remain under-researched. To gain a deeper understanding of such issues, this study focuses on older people living in Kenyan informal settlements and uses the capability approach to investigate their options for mobility, activities and locations, barriers, coping strategies and unfulfilled mobility needs. The study employs a qualitative approach comprising four focus groups with 24 participants and a follow-on community engagement workshop with people aged above 50 years old living in the informal settlement of Kibera in Nairobi. The results indicate that older people face significant challenges in their everyday mobility. Living with poor health conditions, level of poverty, lack and poor quality of walking infrastructure, personal safety and ageist behaviour are the main factors shaping and affecting their mobility. The capability approach offers a framework to understand older people’s mobility practices and implications for wellbeing. It enables an analysis of how mobility is shaped by their needs, constraints and decision-making processes, and how this translates into participation in life. It also offers an exploration of how mobility enables and supports a wider capability set, outlining its multidimensional social impacts.

articles.title: Who Governs Cyberspace in the age of AI?  A Global Stocktake of Cybersecurity Governance
articles.authors: YANNIS KEMEL
articles.abstract:
Cyberspace is not ungoverned—it is systematically misgoverned. International instruments, national strategies, and voluntary frameworks have proliferated while security outcomes deteriorated. This inversion—more governance, less resilience—is the "Proliferation Paradox." The paper introduces "Governance Debt": the institutional deficit that accumulates when AI-driven technological change outpaces governance adaptation. This debt compounds across three dimensions: Capacity Debt (threat velocity exceeds institutional response cycles), Authority Debt (no enforcement jurisdiction over borderless AI infrastructure), and Legitimacy Debt (governance authority migrates to unaccountable private actors).
This paper examines cybersecurity governance in 29 countries. It identifies three institutional models: Integrated Command, Nodal Hybrid, and Fragmented Sectoral. Key finding: institutional architecture predicts operational resilience more consistently than strategy quality or financial investment. The analysis covers 66 governance instruments and reveals four critical weaknesses in how AI challenges current systems: Scale (velocity), Attribution (identity), Dependence (vulnerability to foreign platforms), and Agency (autonomous systems without accountability). The paper calls cloud providers, AI developers, and standards bodies "private leviathans": they exercise sovereign-scale power over cyberspace without democratic mandate or meaningful accountability.
Using the OECD Strategic Foresight Toolkit, the paper stress-tests six governance models across four 2030 scenarios. Two extremes fail: total centralization (a Global Cyber Agency) and total market-led governance. The "Adaptive Federated Stack"—a five-layer architecture—distributes authority across existing institutions without creating a new apex body. This model performs across all scenarios and resolves all three Governance Debt dimensions. The central argument: between 2026 and 2030, governance success requires interoperability, not unity.

articles.title: Affective Political Communication and Voters' Choice of Candidates during Elections
articles.authors: Adriel Ekozin; Bertrand Ojeh
articles.abstract: Political campaigns in modern day democracies have increasingly relied on emotional appeals, symbolic messaging and identity- based narratives to mobilise voters and shape public opinion. This study examined affective political communication and voters' choices of candidates during elections. Affective political communication involves the strategic use of emotions such as fear, hope, anger and enthusiasm to influence political attitudes and behaviours. In many political settings, political actors deploy various communication channels including campaign rallies, traditional media and social media platforms to disseminate emotionally framed messages aimed at persuading voters. The researcher adopted the library research method for the study, while the theoretical framework was hinged on Affective Intelligence Theory. Findings from the study revealed that affective political communication plays a significant role in voters' choice of candidates during elections. Also, emotional appeals embedded in campaign messages influences how voters interpret the personality, credibility and leadership qualities of candidates. Further findings revealed that media exposure particularly through social media platforms and political campaign events amplifies affective messages and contribute to the formation of positive or negative voter attitudes toward candidates. From the findings, the researcher recommended among other things, that political actors and campaign organisations adopt responsible and issue- based communication strategies that emphasise credible information rather than manipulative emotional appeals.

articles.title: Monetary-Fiscal Nexus and Price Level Dynamics in Ghana: An ARDL Analysis of Public Debt Servicing, Short-Term Debt, and Inflation (1980-2020)
articles.authors: Isaac Nyame; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
This study investigates the macroeconomic consequences of public debt composition and servicing obligations on price stability in Ghana from 1980 to 2020. Operating within a dual-model framework, the empirical analysis distinguishes between the cash-flow effects of public debt servicing (PDS) and the maturity-risk impacts of short-term public debt (SPD). The econometric methodology employs Dickey-Fuller Generalised Least Squares (DF-GLS) and Phillips-Perron unit root tests alongside an Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) bounds testing approach to accommodate mixed integration orders among the variables. The bounds test confirms a stable, long-run cointegrating relationship in both the PDS and SPD specifications.
Empirically, the study reveals that PDS exerts a statistically significant negative effect on domestic inflation in both the short run (coefficient = -0.81, p < 0.10) and the long run (coefficient = -0.93, p < 0.05). Conversely, SPD remains statistically insignificant across both temporal horizons. These findings challenge the conventional predictions of the fiscal theory of the price level, suggesting instead that heavy debt-servicing requirements contract aggregate demand by crowding out productive public expenditure. Among the control variables, exchange rate depreciation acts as the dominant positive driver of price levels, while economic growth serves as a robust dampening factor. The error correction terms (-0.87 and -0.90) indicate a rapid speed of adjustment toward long-run equilibrium. These findings suggest that debt restructuring should be coupled with careful demand management, and emphasize that stabilizing local currency value should be prioritized over adjusting debt maturity profiles to maintain overall economic stability.

articles.title: Demand estimation and pricing of perishable food
articles.authors: KOHEI HAYASHIDA
articles.abstract: Demand estimation of perishable food products is challenging because freshness is typically not recorded in available data. To address this measurement challenge, I collect novel customer-transaction and batch-level-production panels—enabling direct observation of freshness for each individual unit in both consumer purchases and choice sets—from the prepared fried-food category of a Japanese grocery chain. With this unique data, I study consumer willingness-to-pay for freshness, its joint heterogeneous distribution with price sensitivity, and grocers’ optimal pricing by freshness using improved demand estimation. I build and estimate a model of consumer batch choice (i.e., freshness) for perishable goods, capturing heterogeneous freshness preferences and price sensitivity. I find that consumer demand for freshness is substantial, with an additional hour of freshness for an average product equivalent to 4.0% of its full price, but freshness preferences also correlate with price sensitivity, implying an absence of purely price-driven consumers. I then model the retailer’s dynamic markdown problem—trading-off discount losses against future stockout losses—and show that optimal markdowns can be zero under observed heterogeneity and the production process. I implement the proposed policy in the focal store; the structural model predicts a focal profit increase of 2.24% (95% posterior predictive interval [1.91, 2.60]), and a field implementation yields a profit increase of about 6.3%, consistent with this prediction. My findings can explain cross-grocer differences in pricing-by-freshness policies as a function of consumer heterogeneity.

articles.title: Data Centres and the Emergence of the Twin Transition Industrial Complex
articles.authors: Giorgos Bougioukos; Charis Papaevangelou; Eugenia Siapera
articles.abstract: The EU has called the ‘twin transition’ a win-win strategy for tackling climate change and strengthening its digital autonomy amid geopolitical and geoeconomic tensions. However, high-level policy discourse can hide the material frictions, extractive dynamics, and emerging dependencies associated with implementing this agenda. The paper examines Western Macedonia, a region historically defined by lignite extraction, now transforming into Greece’s post-lignite development hub. Drawing on critical data centre studies and critical political economy scholarship, we trace the emergence of a twin transition industrial complex (TTIC), that is a system of a parallel and interlinked development of renewables and large-scale, often AI-ready data centres. Methodologically, the study combines 12 semi-structured interviews with locals, on-site visits, as well as an analysis of relevant policy and corporate communication documents. We discern four key findings: A) Western Macedonia is transformed into a hub for renewable energy and data centres, which will help Greece integrate into the global AI value chain. B) However, this shift is causing the displacement of locals, especially young people, and creating a ‘limbo’ situation that will lead to social and ecological exhaustion. C) The green transition is also creating ‘green enclosures’ and transforming the landscape by converting land, including historically publicly funded PPC land, into assets for a more exclusionary and extractive digital transformation. D) Despite official narratives portraying Western Macedonia as a ‘Greek Silicon Valley’, locals are excluded from decision-making and governance processes, leading to disillusionment and a sense of hopelessness for the future. Our main contribution is the outlining of an emerging twin transition industrial complex, wherein the twin transition and digital transformation are planned and materialised by state and (Big Tech) capitalist actors according to the imperatives of the AI industry’s global value chains at the expense of local communities and ecosystems.

articles.title: The Future of Sex Work Legal Models, Platform Economy, and Labour Organisation in International Comparison. A Theoretical Synthesis.
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work along
three interlocking axes: legal governance models, platformisation, and labour
organisation. Drawing exclusively on peer-reviewed research and
methodologically transparent evaluations - including the systematic review by
Platt et al. (2018), the Lancet series on HIV and sex work (Shannon et al.
2015), the New Zealand evaluation literature following the Prostitution
Reform Act 2003, the German federal evaluation of the
Prostituiertenschutzgesetz (KFN 2025), and emerging implementation
research on Belgium's 2024 labour law framework - it argues that the future of
sex work will be decided less in criminal law than in labour law and digital
infrastructure. The paper identifies a structural paradox: while the
epidemiological and criminological evidence increasingly supports
decriminalisation and labour rights, the political momentum in Europe,
reinforced by the European Parliament's 2023 resolution and the European
Court of Human Rights' 2024 ruling on the French client criminalisation law,
points in the opposite direction. Simultaneously, platform capitalism and
financial infrastructure exercise a form of private, extraterritorial regulation -
exemplified by FOSTA-SESTA's global chilling effects - that increasingly
overrides national legal frameworks. Three scenarios for 2040 are developed:
labour-law normalisation (the Belgian path), neo-abolitionist expansion, and
platform-mediated informalisation. The paper concludes that the third
scenario is currently the most probable default, and that only deliberate
institutional design - of the kind pioneered in Belgium - can shift the
trajectory.

Canonical German version https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/df43j_v1

articles.title: Visible Threats, Invisible Lives: A Constructivist Grounded Theory of Non-Heteronormative Visibility Management in Qazaqstan
articles.authors: Dinara Pisareva; Nurmakhan Tastaibek; Aruzhan Auyez; Aruzhan Kaparova; Diana Molzhigit; Satybaldy; Nikita Durnev; Maiya Talipova
articles.abstract: In 2025, Qazaqstan passed a law banning LGBT “propaganda.” Among other things, it raised the question of how Qazaqstanis construct meaning around issues related to sexual and gender minorities (SGM). Our paper answers this question through a constructivist grounded theory analysis of 808 survey responses and six follow-up interviews. We find that in Qazaqstan the boundary between tolerating and opposing queer people is often drawn at how visible they are. Our results show that ordinary people are as concerned with non-heteronormative visibility management as queer people are. We identify three meaning-making frameworks that respondents use when engaging with questions about sexual and gender minorities: a visibility-contingent framework (77%), a principled-acceptance framework (14%), and an existence-rejection framework (6%). Respondents in the visibility-contingent framework make sense of SGM by deliberating how much visibility is allowed and on which terms. We also show that language strongly shapes the discursive repertoire of our respondents. Qazaq speakers express more negative attitudes (71% vs. 37%) and often see queer visibility as a threat, while Russian speakers talk about private sexuality and conditional tolerance. Overall, these findings assist in explaining popular support for the propaganda law, whose non-heteronormative visibility logic mirrors our respondents’ own.

articles.title: Pensamiento Periférico y Poder Periférico. Una reflexión conjunta necesaria.
articles.authors: Andrés Miguel Figueroa Jiménez
articles.abstract:
E. Devés en su teoría Pensamiento Periférico plantea una tesis interpretativa global para entender, desde las arquitecturas y despliegues de ideas a los desarrollos multinivel de agentes periféricos en el pasado, en el presente y también para dar claridad a los intentos societales de estos agentes por construir horizontes de futuro. El núcleo analítico de Devés consiste en el siguiente razonamiento empíricamente sostenido: “La manera más comprensiva de entender el pensamiento emergido en las regiones periféricas durante los últimos siglos –regiones en general sometidas a procesos de invasión o colonización– consiste en la disyuntiva: ser-como-el-centro versus ser-nosotros-mismos. Este problema se lo ha planteado la intelectualidad latinoamericana, la africana y la asiática, como también la eslava, la balcánica y la ibérica de esta época”. No obstante, en su desarrollo, la propuesta de Devés muestra, a nuestro juicio, un flanco abierto, una “Grieta analítica” que impactaría en su performance como insumo explicativo e interpretativo. Este flanco abierto es la débil o casi inexistente incorporación a la teoría de Devés de una reflexión sobre el poder y particularmente respecto del poder de los agentes que su obra pretende sacar a flote, a saber: los agentes periféricos, débiles y justamente los más sensibles a los efectos de formulaciones y acciones de poder. Ante la problemática expuesta, se presenta como alternativa para una colaboración analítica, para una reflexión conjunta, la categoría: Poder Periférico. Lo anterior, a través de la explicitación de sus componentes (principios justificativos, características fundamentales y condiciones generales), sus dimensiones y sus intensidades. Para este ejercicio son elegidos con fines solamente ilustrativos 5 gobiernos de Chile entre (1952-1990). En esta operación disciplinar coordinada, se provocan avances hacia el cierre o disminución de la grieta analítica y se articula el inicio de una comunión entre la teoría Pensamiento Periférico y el concepto de Poder Periférico que fortalece al desarrollo de tareas explicativas y comprensivas y sus resultados respecto a la realidad de agentes débiles y de los espacios periféricos.

Palabras claves: A. Figueroa, E. Devés, Grieta analítica, Pensamiento Periférico, Poder Periférico.

articles.title: Beyond Augmentation: Epistemic Complementarity in LLM-Assisted Qualitative Analysis
articles.authors: Alina Ionela Palimaru; Ryan Brown; Daniel Siconolfi; Michael S. Dunbar; Nipher Malika; Prateek Puri; Elizabeth J. D’Amico
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly entering qualitative analysis workflows, yet the field's discussion of their integration has unfolded as though "qualitative research" were a single tradition with a single epistemological stake. Drawing on a review of fifty-one studies on LLM-assisted qualitative analysis published between 2024 and 2026, we observe that approximately 80% do not articulate an epistemological stance, while those that do rarely work within their stated paradigm as a constraint on methodological decisions. The result is a debate that conflates important issues: interpretivist scholars mount forceful objections to LLM use that may not extend to applied qualitative work, while pragmatic integrators describe sophisticated workflows without consistently operationalizing the standards by which their tradition assesses analytic adequacy. In this article, we argue that the integration question is methodology-specific and must be answered on the terms each tradition sets for itself. We then offer a worked answer for one pathway through the qualitative research landscape: a pragmatic narrative inquiry combining Framework Analysis with Codebook Thematic Analysis, drawn from the CHAT project, a PCORI-funded study of adolescent substance use involving 86 semi-structured interviews and 5,466 coded excerpts analyzed through Muse QDA, a purpose-built LLM-assisted qualitative analysis platform. Our framework rests on three principles: epistemic capacity, which specifies what each agent (human, LLM) is positioned to claim at each phase of analysis; analytic significance, which distinguishes calibration interventions (instrument-tuning) from meaning-making interventions (analysis-shaping) and locates the latter at tradition-specific decision points; and trajectory documentation, which preserves the path of analytic judgment without claiming reproducibility of outputs. The framework's distinctive theoretical contribution is a strict form of complementarity: human and LLM epistemic capacities are non-substitutable within this workflow, such that neither agent can do the other's work without epistemic loss. We illustrate the framework through a three-track codebook development process, a documented case of conceptual sharpening between two parent codes, and a "What did I miss?" exercise in which LLM coverage prompted human meaning-making.
